title: Documentation
text: The pfas_birthweight package provides a suite of functions for loading, cleaning, merging, and analyzing PFAS drinking water contamination data alongside CDC birth weight outcomes. This page documents all available functions.

title: Documentation
text: The pfas_birthweight package provides a suite of functions for loading, cleaning, merging, and analyzing PFAS drinking water contamination data alongside CDC birth weight outcomes. This page documents all available functions.

title: Documentation
text:
Pipeline Functions

build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
The primary function that runs the entire pipeline end-to-end and returns a county-year panel ready for analysis.
Signature:
build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: A panel DataFrame with the following columns:




Column
Description




FIPS
5-digit county FIPS code


STATE
2-letter state abbreviation


COUNTY_SERVED
County name


year
Year (2016–2024)


births
Number of births


avg_birth_weight
Average birth weight (grams)


PFAS_county
Population-weighted mean PFAS concentration (ng/L)



Example:
from pfas_birthweight import build_pfas_birthweight_panel

panel = build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
print(panel.head())



load_pfas()
Loads and cleans the raw PFAS sample data from the bundled Excel file, replacing non-detects with MRL/2.
Signature:
load_pfas()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Cleaned sample-level PFAS data with a result column in ng/L




collapse_pfas_to_pws()
Aggregates sample-level PFAS measurements to one row per public water system.
Signature:
collapse_pfas_to_pws(pfas_df)
Parameters:

pfas_df (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_pfas()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: One row per PWS ID with mean PFAS concentration and population served




load_geo()
Loads the EPA SDWA Geographic Areas file, keeping only county-level service area rows (AREA_TYPE_CODE == "CN").
Signature:
load_geo()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Filtered geographic areas with PWSID and COUNTY_SERVED columns




build_county_pfas()
Merges PWS-level PFAS data with the county mapping and computes population-weighted average PFAS per county.
Signature:
build_county_pfas(pws_pfas, geo_df)
Parameters:

pws_pfas (pd.DataFrame): Output of collapse_pfas_to_pws()
geo_df (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_geo()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: One row per (STATE, COUNTY_SERVED) with a PFAS_county column




load_crosswalk()
Loads the Census national county FIPS crosswalk and standardizes county names by removing suffixes like “County”, “City”, and “Parish”.
Signature:
load_crosswalk()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Crosswalk with STATE, FIPS, COUNTY_NAME, and COUNTY_CLEAN columns




attach_fips_to_pfas()
Joins 5-digit FIPS codes onto the county-level PFAS data using the Census crosswalk.
Signature:
attach_fips_to_pfas(county_pfas, crosswalk)
Parameters:

county_pfas (pd.DataFrame): Output of build_county_pfas()
crosswalk (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_crosswalk()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: county_pfas with a FIPS column attached




load_birth_weights()
Loads the CDC WONDER birth weight data, dropping suppressed cells and CDC footer rows.
Signature:
load_birth_weights()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Panel data with columns FIPS, year, births, and avg_birth_weight




build_panel()
Merges the birth weight panel with county-level PFAS exposure data on FIPS code.
Signature:
build_panel(bw_df, pfas_fips)
Parameters:

bw_df (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_birth_weights()
pfas_fips (pd.DataFrame): Output of attach_fips_to_pfas()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Final panel sorted by FIPS and year

title: Documentation
text:
Analysis Functions

plot_eda()
Produces two side-by-side exploratory plots of the panel data.
Signature:
plot_eda(panel)
Parameters:

panel (pd.DataFrame): Output of build_pfas_birthweight_panel()

Plots produced:

Scatter plot of PFAS concentration vs average birth weight with an OLS trend line
Average birth weight over time (2016–2024) by state

Example:
from pfas_birthweight import build_pfas_birthweight_panel, plot_eda

panel = build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
plot_eda(panel)



run_regression()
Runs an OLS regression of average birth weight on PFAS concentration with year fixed effects and standard errors clustered by county.
Signature:
run_regression(panel)
Parameters:

panel (pd.DataFrame): Output of build_pfas_birthweight_panel()

Returns:

RegressionResultsWrapper: A fitted statsmodels results object

Model:
\[\text{BirthWeight}_{ct} = \beta \cdot \text{PFAS}_{c} + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{ct}\]
Where \(\gamma_t\) represents year fixed effects. County fixed effects are excluded by design since PFAS exposure is time-invariant and would be fully absorbed.
Example:
from pfas_birthweight import build_pfas_birthweight_panel, run_regression

panel = build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
model = run_regression(panel)

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
This project investigates the relationship between PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination in public drinking water systems and birth weight outcomes at the county level. Using data from the EPA, Census Bureau, and CDC, we construct a county-year panel dataset covering six states (Alabama, Arkansas, Missouri, New Jersey, Ohio, and Virginia) from 2016 to 2024.
Explore the data interactively with our Streamlit app here!
View our GitHub Repo here!

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
This project investigates the relationship between PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination in public drinking water systems and birth weight outcomes at the county level. Using data from the EPA, Census Bureau, and CDC, we construct a county-year panel dataset covering six states (Alabama, Arkansas, Missouri, New Jersey, Ohio, and Virginia) from 2016 to 2024.
Explore the data interactively with our Streamlit app here!
View our GitHub Repo here!

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
Data Sources

EPA UCMR PFAS Monitoring Data: Sample-level PFAS measurements (ng/L) from public water systems across the six study states
EPA SDWA Geographic Areas: Maps public water system IDs to the counties they serve
Census National County FIPS Crosswalk: Links county names to standardized 5-digit FIPS codes
CDC WONDER Birth Weight Data: County-level average birth weight and total births by year (2016–2024)

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
Pipeline
The pipeline is packaged as pfas-birthweight, available on PyPI. It can be installed and run with:
pip install pfas-birthweight

from pfas_birthweight import build_pfas_birthweight_panel
panel = build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
The pipeline runs through the following steps:

Load and clean PFAS data: Raw sample-level measurements are loaded and non-detects are replaced with half the minimum reporting level (MRL/2), a standard method in environmental health literature
Collapse to PWS level: Samples are averaged to the public water system level, retaining population served
Merge with EPA geographic areas: PWS IDs are matched to counties using the EPA SDWA geographic areas dataset, filtering for county-level service area codes
Aggregate to county level: PFAS concentrations are aggregated to the county level using population-weighted averages, grouping by state and county to avoid collapsing same-named counties across states
Attach FIPS codes: County names are standardized and matched to 5-digit FIPS codes using the Census crosswalk
Merge with birth weight data: The county-level PFAS estimates are merged onto the CDC WONDER panel using FIPS codes, producing a county-year dataset ready for analysis

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
Analysis
The final dataset contains 404 unique counties across 6 states with 747 county-year observations. Analysis is performed using OLS regression with year fixed effects and standard errors clustered by county:
\[\text{BirthWeight}_{ct} = \beta \cdot \text{PFAS}_{c} + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{ct}\]
Where \(\gamma_t\) represents year fixed effects controlling for national trends in birth weight over time. Because PFAS exposure is measured as a single cross-sectional value per county, county fixed effects are excluded by design as they would absorb the exposure variable entirely.

title: Technical Report
text: This report documents the workflow used to collect, clean, enrich, and explore the General Conference talk dataset. It is intended to make the analysis reproducible by showing the full path from the raw Church website pages to the final CSV and downstream exploration.

title: Technical Report
text: This report documents the workflow used to collect, clean, enrich, and explore the General Conference talk dataset. It is intended to make the analysis reproducible by showing the full path from the raw Church website pages to the final CSV and downstream exploration.

title: Technical Report
text:
1. Data Extraction
The collection process is implemented in the src/gc_analysis/data.py module. The main entry point is data_pipeline(), which loops over the requested years and conference months and calls grab_gc_talk() for each pair.
The extraction step follows this sequence:

Request the General Conference index page for a specific year and month.
Parse the HTML with BeautifulSoup.
Locate each talk link on the conference page.
Visit each talk page individually.
Extract the title, speaker, role, kicker, and body text.

The collected fields are stored in a dictionary first and then converted into a pandas DataFrame. That approach keeps the extraction logic simple and makes the output easy to inspect before later processing.

title: Technical Report
text:
2. Data Structure
The raw dataset produced by the pipeline contains these columns:

title
author
role
kicker
text
year
month

After embeddings are generated, an additional embedding column is added.
This structure is important because it separates what was scraped from what was derived later. Anyone rerunning the pipeline can compare the raw text fields against cleaned or embedded versions without guessing how the data changed.

title: Technical Report
text:
3. Cleaning And Standardization
Once the talks are collected, clean_data() standardizes the role field. The source pages use full role names, but the analysis uses compact labels such as A for Apostle and RS for Relief Society.
This step reduces variation in the dataset and makes grouping, counting, and plotting more reliable. It also keeps role-based summaries readable when exploring the collection.

title: Technical Report
text:
4. Embedding Generation
The next step is semantic enrichment. generate_embeddings() sends each non-null talk text value to Ollama using the embeddinggemma model and stores the returned vector as a JSON string.
Why this matters:

It makes similarity analysis possible.
It supports clustering and search-style exploration.
It preserves the vectors in CSV form so the dataset can be reloaded without recomputing everything immediately.

The embeddings are saved as text for compatibility with CSV storage, and load_embeddings_from_csv() later converts them back into numeric arrays.

title: Technical Report
text:
5. Persisting The Output
When save_csv=True, the pipeline writes the final dataset to data/gc_talks.csv. That file becomes the reproducible artifact for the rest of the project.
To recreate the dataset, rerun the pipeline with the same years and months arguments. The defaults collect April and October 2025, but the inputs can be changed to reproduce the exact slice used in any analysis.
Example:
from gc_analysis.data import data_pipeline

df = data_pipeline(years=[2025], months=["04", "10"], save_csv=True, verbose=True)

title: Technical Report
text:
6. Exploration Workflow
After the CSV is created, the dataset can be explored with pandas or any downstream notebook or app. A typical exploration sequence is:

Load the CSV with load_embeddings_from_csv().
Check dataset size, unique speakers, and role counts.
Review talk titles, summaries, and speakers for quality control.
Filter by year, month, author, or role.
Use embeddings for similarity search or broader thematic exploration.

Example exploration code:
from gc_analysis.data import load_embeddings_from_csv

df = load_embeddings_from_csv("data/gc_talks.csv")

print(len(df))
print(df["role"].value_counts())
print(df[["title", "author", "role"]].head())

title: Technical Report
text:
7. Reproducibility Notes
To reproduce the analysis reliably, the following assumptions should be kept consistent:

The same source site structure is available on the Church website.
Ollama is installed and running locally.
The embeddinggemma model is available.
The same year and month inputs are used when calling the pipeline.

If any of those inputs change, the resulting dataset may differ. The pipeline is intentionally explicit about these dependencies so future runs can be traced back to the original extraction and enrichment steps.

title: Technical Report
text:
8. Summary
The project workflow is straightforward: scrape the talks, standardize the metadata, generate embeddings, save the dataset, and then explore the result from a reproducible CSV. This page is the narrative record of that process.

title: Getting Started
text:
Install the package directly from PyPI:
pip install pfas-birthweight
The package requires Python 3.9+ and will automatically install the following dependencies:

pandas - data manipulation
numpy - numerical operations
openpyxl - Excel file reading
statsmodels - regression analysis
matplotlib - plotting

title: Getting Started
text:
Install the package directly from PyPI:
pip install pfas-birthweight
The package requires Python 3.9+ and will automatically install the following dependencies:

pandas - data manipulation
numpy - numerical operations
openpyxl - Excel file reading
statsmodels - regression analysis
matplotlib - plotting

title: Getting Started
text:
Quick Start

1. Build the Panel
All data is bundled with the package. Simply run:
from pfas_birthweight import build_pfas_birthweight_panel

panel = build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
print(panel.head())
This will:

Load and clean raw PFAS sample data, replacing non-detects with MRL/2
Collapse measurements to the public water system level
Merge with EPA geographic areas to map systems to counties
Aggregate to population-weighted county-level PFAS estimates
Attach 5-digit FIPS codes using the Census crosswalk
Merge with CDC WONDER birth weight data by FIPS and year



2. Explore the Data
# Check coverage
print(f"Counties: {panel['FIPS'].nunique()}")
print(f"States:   {panel['STATE'].unique()}")
print(f"Years:    {sorted(panel['year'].unique())}")

# View a specific state
print(panel[panel['STATE'] == 'OH'].head())
The resulting panel has the following columns:



Column
Description




FIPS
5-digit county FIPS code


STATE
2-letter state abbreviation


COUNTY_SERVED
County name


year
Year (2016–2024)


births
Number of births


avg_birth_weight
Average birth weight (grams)


PFAS_county
Population-weighted mean PFAS (ng/L)





3. Plot the Data
from pfas_birthweight import plot_eda

plot_eda(panel)
This produces two side-by-side plots:

A scatter plot of PFAS concentration vs average birth weight with a trend line
Average birth weight over time (2016–2024) by state



4. Run the Regression
from pfas_birthweight import run_regression

model = run_regression(panel)
Runs an OLS regression of average birth weight on PFAS concentration with year fixed effects and standard errors clustered by county:
\[\text{BirthWeight}_{ct} = \beta \cdot \text{PFAS}_{c} + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{ct}\]

title: Getting Started
text:
Saving the Panel
If you plan to run analysis scripts repeatedly, save the panel to CSV to avoid rebuilding the pipeline each time:
panel.to_csv("panel.csv", index=False)
Then load it directly in subsequent scripts:
import pandas as pd
panel = pd.read_csv("panel.csv", dtype={"FIPS": str})
